62	---
63	
64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179	
180	### What you MUST do
181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.

281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.

3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

2154	\section{Construction of Quot Schemes}
2155	
2156	\medskip
2157	
2158	
2159	
2160	\centerline{\large\bf Notions of Projectivity}
2161	
2162	\medskip
2163	
2164	
2165	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme. Recall that as defined by Grothendieck, 
2166	a morphism $X\to S$ is called a \textbf{projective morphism}
2167	if there exists a coherent sheaf $E$ on $S$, together with a 
2168	closed embedding of $X$ into $\PP(E) = \Proj \sym_{{\mathcal O}_S} E $ 
2169	over $S$. Equivalently, $X\to S$ is projective when 
2170	it is proper and there exists a relatively very ample
2171	line bundle $L$ on $X$ over $S$. These conditions are related by
2172	taking $L$ to be the restriction of ${\mathcal O}_{\PP(E)}(1)$ to $X$, or
2173	in the reverse direction, taking $E$ to be the direct image of $L$ on $S$.
2174	A morphism $X\to S$ is called \textbf{quasi-projective} if it factors as 
2175	an open embedding $X\hra Y$ followed by a projective morphism $Y \to S$.
2176	
2177	
2178	A stronger version of projectivity was introduced 
2179	by Altman and Kleiman: a morphism
2180	$X\to S$ of noetherian schemes is called \textbf{strongly projective} 
2181	(respectively, \textbf{strongly quasi-projective}) if
2182	there exists a vector bundle $E$ on $S$ together with a closed
2183	embedding (respectively, a locally closed embedding)
2184	$X\subset \PP(E)$ over $S$. 
2185	
2186	
2187	Finally, the strongest version of (quasi-)projectivity 
2188	is as follows (used for example in
2189	the textbook [H] by Hartshorne): a morphism
2190	$X\to S$ of noetherian schemes is projective in the strongest sense 
2191	if $X$ admits a (locally-)closed embedding into $\PP^n_S$ for some $n$.
2192	
2193	Note that none of the three versions of projectivity
2194	is local over the base $S$.
2195	
2196	\medskip
2197	
2198	\textbf{Exercises } (i) Gives examples to show that the above
2199	three notions of projectivity are in general distinct. 
2200	
2201	(ii) Show that if $X\to S$ is projective and flat, where $S$ is noetherian,
2202	then $X\to S$ is strongly projective.
2203	
2204	(iii) Note that if every coherent sheaf of ${\mathcal O}_S$-modules
2205	is the quotient of a vector bundle, then projectivity over the base $S$ 
2206	is equivalent to strong projectivity. If $S$ admits an ample line bundle 
2207	(for example, if $S$ is quasi-projective over an affine base), 
2208	then all three notions of projectivity over $S$ 
2209	are equivalent to each other.

2220	\centerline{\large\bf Main Existence Theorems}
2221	
2222	
2223	\medskip
2224	
2225	
2226	
2227	Grothendieck's original theorem on Quot schemes, 
2228	whose proof is outlined in [FGA] TDTE-IV, is the following.
2229	
2230	\begin{theorem}\label{Grothendieck Quot} {\rm (Grothendieck)}  
2231	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $\pi: X\to S$ a projective morphism,
2232	and $L$ a relatively very ample line bundle on $X$. Then for any 
2233	coherent ${\mathcal O}_X$-module $E$ and any polynomial $\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]$,
2234	the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is representable
2235	by a projective $S$-scheme $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$.
2236	\end{theorem}
2237	
2238	
2239	Altman and Kleiman gave a complete and detailed proof of the existence of
2240	Quot schemes in [A-K 2]. They could remove the noetherian hypothesis, 
2241	by instead assuming strong (quasi-)projectivity of $X\to S$ 
2242	together with an assumption about the nature of the coherent  
2243	sheaf $E$, and deduce that the scheme $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is then
2244	strongly (quasi-)projective over $S$.
2245	
2246	For simplicity, in these lecture notes we state and prove the result in [A-K 2]
2247	in the noetherian context.
2248	
2249	\begin{theorem}\label{Altman-Kleiman Quot} {\rm (Altman-Kleiman)}
2250	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $X$ a closed subscheme of 
2251	$\PP(V)$ for some vector bundle $V$ on $S$, $L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1)|_X$,
2252	$E$ a coherent quotient sheaf of $\pi^*(W)(\nu)$ where
2253	$W$ is a vector bundle on $S$ and $\nu$ is an integer, and 
2254	$\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]$. 
2255	Then the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is representable
2256	by a scheme $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ which can be embedded over $S$ as a 
2257	closed subscheme of $\PP(F)$ for some vector bundle $F$ on $S$.
2258	
2259	The vector bundle $F$ can be chosen to be an exterior power of 
2260	the tensor product of $W$ with a symmetric powers of $V$.
2261	\end{theorem}
2262	
2263	Taking both $V$ and $W$ to be trivial in the above, we get the following.
2264	
2265	\begin{theorem}
2266	If $S$ is a noetherian scheme, $X$ is a closed subscheme of 
2267	$\PP^n_S$ for some $n\ge 0$, $L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S}(1)|_X$,
2268	$E$ is a coherent quotient sheaf of $\oplus^p {\mathcal O}_X(\nu)$ 
2269	for some integers $p\ge 0$ and $\nu$, and $\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]$,
2270	then the the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is representable
2271	by a scheme $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ which can be embedded over $S$ as a 
2272	closed subscheme of $\PP^r_S$ for some $r\ge 0$.
2273	\end{theorem}
2274	
2275	
2276	The rest of this section is devoted to proving 
2277	Theorem \ref{Altman-Kleiman Quot}, with extra noetherian hypothesis.
2278	At the end, we will remark on how the proof also
2279	gives us the original version of Grothendieck.
2280

2288	\centerline{\large\bf Reduction to the case of 
2289	$\Quot_{\pi^*W/\PP(V)/S}^{\Phi,L}$}
2290	
2291	\medskip
2292	
2293	
2294	It is enough to prove Theorem \ref{Altman-Kleiman Quot} in the special case
2295	that $X = \PP(V)$ and
2296	$E= \pi^*(W)$ where $V$ and $W$ are vector bundles on $S$, as
2297	a consequence of the next lemma.
2298	
2299	\begin{lemma}
2300	(i) Let $\nu$ be any integer. 
2301	Then tensoring by $L^{\nu}$ gives an isomorphism of
2302	functors from $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ to 
2303	$\Quot_{E(\nu)/X/S}^{\Psi,L}$ where the polynomial 
2304	$\Psi \in \Q[\lambda]$ is defined by $\Psi(\lambda) = \Phi(\lambda + \nu)$. 
2305	
2306	(ii) Let $\phi : E \to G$ be a surjective homomorphism of 
2307	coherent sheaves on $X$. Then the corresponding natural transformation
2308	$\Quot_{G/X/S}^{\Phi,L} \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$
2309	is a closed embedding.
2310	\end{lemma}
2311	
2312	\proof The statement (i) is obvious. The statement (ii) just says that
2313	given any locally noetherian scheme $T$ and a family
2314	$\langle \F,q\rangle \in \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(T)$, there exists
2315	a closed subscheme $T' \subset T$ with the following universal property:
2316	for any locally noetherian scheme $U$ and a morphism $f: U\to T$,
2317	the pulled back homomorphism of ${\mathcal O}_{X_U}$-modules $q_U : E_U \to \F_U$
2318	factors via the pulled back homomorphism $\phi_U : E_U \to G_U$
2319	if and only if $U \to T$ factors via $T'\hra T$. 
2320	This is satisfied by taking $T'$ to be the vanishing scheme
2321	for the composite homomorphism $\ker(\phi) \hra  E \stackrel{q}{\to} \F$
2322	of coherent sheaves on $X_T$ (see Remark \ref{vanishing scheme}), 
2323	which makes sense here as both $\ker(\phi)$ and $\F$ are coherent 
2324	on $X_T$ and $\F$ is flat over $T$. \qed
2325	
2326	
2327	
2328	Therefore if $\Quot_{\pi^*W/\PP(V)/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is
2329	representable, then for any coherent quotient
2330	$E$ of $\pi^*W(\nu)|_X$, we can take $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ 
2331	to be a closed subscheme of $\quot_{\pi^*W/\PP(V)/S}^{\Phi,L}$.

2409	\centerline{\large\bf Embedding Quot into Grassmannian}
2410	
2411	\medskip
2412	
2413	We now fix a positive integer $r$ such that $r\ge m$.
2414	Note that the rank of ${\pi_T}_*\F(r)$ is $\Phi(r)$
2415	and $\pi_*E(r) = W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V$.
2416	Therefore the surjective homomorphism
2417	${\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)$
2418	defines an element of the set 
2419	$\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V , \Phi(r))(T)$.
2420	We thus get a morphism of functors 
2421	$$\alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to 
2422	\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$$
2423	It associates to $q: E_T \to \F$ the quotient
2424	${\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)$.
2425	 
2426	The above morphism $\alpha$ is injective because the quotient 
2427	$q: E_T \to \F$ can be recovered from 
2428	${\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)$ 
2429	as follows. 
2430	
2431	If $G = \grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$ with
2432	projection $p_G : G \to S$, and
2433	$u : {p_G}^*E \to \U$ denotes the universal quotient on $G$
2434	with kernel $v : \K \to {p_G}^*E$,
2435	then the homomorphism 
2436	${\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(\G(r))\to {\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*E_T(r)$ 
2437	can be recovered 
2438	from the morphism $T \to G$ as
2439	the pull-back of $v : \K \to {p_G}^*E$.
2440	Let $h$ be  the composite 
2441	${\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(\G(r))\to {\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(E_T(r))\to E_T(r)$.
2442	As a consequence of the properties of the diagram \textbf{(**)}, 
2443	the following is a right exact sequence on $X_T$
2444	$${\pi_T}^*{\pi_T}_*(\G(r))\stackrel{h}{\to} 
2445	E_T(r) \stackrel{q(r)}{\to} \F \to 0$$
2446	and so $q(r) : E_T(r) \to \F(r)$ can be recovered as the cokernel of $h$.
2447	Finally, twisting by $-r$, we recover $q$, proving the desired 
2448	injectivity of the morphism of functors 
2449	$\alpha :\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to 
2450	\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$.

2456	\centerline{\large\bf Use of Flattening Stratification}
2457	\nopagebreak
2458	\medskip
2459	\nopagebreak
2460	We will next prove that 
2461	$\alpha :\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to 
2462	\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$
2463	is relatively representable. In fact, we will show that
2464	given any locally noetherian $S$-scheme $T$ and
2465	a surjective homomorphism $f :  W_T \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_T}\sym^r V_T \to \J$
2466	where $\J$ is a locally free ${\mathcal O}_T$-module of rank $\Phi(r)$,
2467	there exists a locally closed subscheme $T'$ of 
2468	$T$ with the following universal property \textbf{(F)} : 
2469	
2470	\textbf{(F) } Given any 
2471	locally noetherian $S$-scheme $Y$ and an $S$-morphism $\phi :Y\to T$,
2472	let $f_Y$ be the pull-back of $f$, and  
2473	let $\K_Y = \ker(f_Y) = \phi^* \ker(f)$. Let
2474	$\pi_Y : X_Y \to Y$ be the projection, and let
2475	$h : {\pi_Y}^* \K_Y \to E_Y$ be the composite map
2476	$${\pi_Y}^* \K_Y \to  {\pi_Y}^* (W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V)
2477	= {\pi_Y}^*{\pi_Y}_*E_Y \to  E_Y$$ 
2478	Let $q:   E_Y \to \F$ be the cokernel of $h$. 
2479	Then $\F$ is flat over $Y$ with its Hilbert polynomial on all fibers
2480	equal to $\Phi$ if and only if $\phi :Y\to T$
2481	factors via $Y'\hra Y$.
2482	
2483	The existence of such a locally closed subscheme $T'$ of 
2484	$T$ is given by Theorem \ref{flattening stratification}, which 
2485	shows that $T'$ is the stratum corresponding to 
2486	Hilbert polynomial $\Phi$ for the 
2487	flattening stratification over $T$ for the sheaf $\F$ on $X_T$.
2488	
2489	When we take $T$ to be $\grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$ 
2490	with universal quotient $u : {p_G}^*E \to \U$, the corresponding
2491	locally closed subscheme $T'$ represents
2492	the functor $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ by its construction.
2493	
2494	Hence we have shown that $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ is represented
2495	by a locally closed subscheme of 
2496	$\grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$. As 
2497	$\grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$ embeds as a 
2498	closed subscheme of $\PP(\bigwedge ^{\Phi(r)} W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V)$,
2499	we get a locally closed embedding of $S$-schemes 
2500	$$\quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \subset 
2501	\PP(\bigwedge ^{\Phi(r)} (W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V))$$
2502	In particular, the morphism $\quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\to S$ 
2503	is separated and of finite type.

2510	\centerline{\large\bf Valuative Criterion for Properness}
2511	
2512	
2513	\medskip
2514	
2515	The original reference for the following argument is EGA IV (2) 2.8.1. 
2516	
2517	The functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ satisfies the following valuative
2518	criterion for properness over $S$: given any discrete valuation ring
2519	$R$ over $S$ with quotient field $K$, the restriction map
2520	$$\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R) \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)$$
2521	is bijective. This can be seen as follows.
2522	Given any coherent quotient 
2523	$q: E_K \to \F$ on $X_R$ which defines an element $\langle \F, q\rangle$
2524	of $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)$.
2525	Let $\ov{\F}$ be the image of the composite homomorphism
2526	$E_R \to j_*(E_K) \to j_*\F$ where $j : X_K \hra X_R$ is the open
2527	inclusion. Let $\ov{q} : E_R\to \ov{\F}$ be the induced surjection.
2528	Then we leave it to the reader to verify that 
2529	$\langle \F, q\rangle$ is an element of $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R)$
2530	which maps to $\langle \F, q\rangle$, and is the unique such element.
2531	(Use the basic fact that being flat over a dvr 
2532	is the same as being torsion-free.)
2533	
2534	As $S$ is noetherian and as we have already shown that 
2535	$\quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\to S$ is of finite type,
2536	it follows that $\quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\to S$ is a proper morphism.
2537	Therefore the embedding of
2538	$\quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ into 
2539	$\PP(\bigwedge ^{\Phi(r)} (W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V))$
2540	is a closed embedding.
2541	
2542	This completes the proof of Theorem \ref{Altman-Kleiman Quot}.\hfill$\square$

2551	\centerline{\large\bf The Version of Grothendieck}
2552	
2553	
2554	\medskip
2555	
2556	
2557	We now describe how to get Theorem \ref{Grothendieck Quot}
2558	from the above proof. As $S$ is noetherian, we can find a common
2559	$m$ such that given any field-valued point 
2560	$s: \spec k\to S$ and a coherent quotient $q: E_s \to \F$ on $X_s$
2561	with Hilbert polynomial $\Phi$,
2562	the sheaves $E_s(r)$, $\F(r)$, $\G(r)$ (where $\G = \ker(q)$) are
2563	generated by global sections and all their higher cohomologies vanish,
2564	whenever $r\ge m$.
2565	This follows from the theory of $m$-regularity, and semi-continuity. 
2566	
2567	Because we have such a common $m$, we get as before an injective morphism
2568	from the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ into the Grassmannian functor
2569	$\Grass(\pi_*E(r), \Phi(r))$. The sheaf 
2570	$\pi_*E(r)$ is coherent, but need not be the quotient of a 
2571	vector bundle on $S$. Consequently, the scheme $\grass(\pi_*E(r), \Phi(r))$
2572	is projective over the base, but not necessarily strongly projective.
2573

2575	made over an affine open cover of $S$, gives

293	\centerline{\large\bf The Functors $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$}
294	
295	
296	\medskip
297	
298	The main problem addressed in this series of lectures, in its simplest
299	form, is as follows. If $S$ is a locally noetherian scheme, 
300	a \textbf{family of subschemes of $\PP^n$ parametrised by $S$} will mean
301	a closed subscheme $Y \subset \PP^n_S = \PP^n_{\Z}\times S$ 
302	such that $Y$ is flat over $S$. 
303	If $f: T\to S$ is any morphism of locally noetherian schemes, then
304	by pull-back we get a family $f^*(Y) = (\id \times f)^{-1}(Y) 
305	\subset \PP^n_T$ 
306	parametrised by $T$, from a family $Y$ parametrised by $S$.
307	This defines a contravariant functor $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$ 
308	from the category of all locally noetherian schemes to the category of sets, 
309	which associates to any $S$ the set of all such families
310	$$\Hilb_{\PP^n}(S) = \{ Y \subset \PP^n_S \,|\,
311	Y \mbox{ is flat over }S \}$$
312	
313	\textbf{Question:} Is the functor $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$ representable?
314	
315	Grothendieck proved that this question has an affirmative answer, that is, 
316	there exists a locally noetherian scheme $\hilb_{\PP^n}$ together with
317	a family $Z \subset \PP^n_{\Z}\times \hilb_{\PP^n}$ 
318	parametrised by $\hilb_{\PP^n}$,
319	such that any family $Y$ over $S$ is obtained as the
320	pull-back of $Z$ 
321	by a uniquely determined morphism $\varphi_Y : S \to \hilb_{\PP^n}$.
322	In other words, $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$ is isomorphic to 
323	the functor $Mor(-,\hilb_{\PP^n})$.
324	
325	
326	
327	
328	
329	\medskip
330	
331	
332	\bigskip
333

335	\centerline{\large\bf The Functors $\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$}
336	\nopagebreak
337	\medskip
338	\nopagebreak
339	A family $Y$ of subschemes of $\PP^n$ parametrised by $S$
340	is the same as 
341	a coherent quotient sheaf $q: {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S} \to {\mathcal O}_Y$ on 
342	$\PP^n_S$, such that ${\mathcal O}_Y$ is flat over $S$. 
343	This way of looking at the functor $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$
344	has the following fruitful generalisation. 
345	
346	
347	Let $r$ be any positive integer. 
348	A \textbf{family of quotients of $\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}$ 
349	parametrised by a locally noetherian scheme $S$} will mean 
350	a pair $(\F,q)$ consisting of 
351	
352	(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $\PP^n_S$ which is flat over $S$, and
353	
354	(ii) a surjective ${\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S}$-linear homomorphism of sheaves 
355	$q:\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S}  \to \F$.
356	
357	Two such families $(\F,q)$ and $(\F,q)$ parametrised by $S$ will 
358	be regarded as equivalent if there exists an isomorphism $f: \F \to \F'$
359	which takes $q$ to $q'$, that is, the following diagram commutes.
360	$$\begin{array}{ccc}
361	\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n} &\stackrel{q}{\to} & \F \\
362	\|                  &     & ~ \dna {\scriptstyle f} \\
363	\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n} &\stackrel{q'}{\to} & \F' 
364	\end{array}$$
365	This is the same as the condition 
366	$\ker(q) = \ker(q')$. We will denote by $\langle \F, q \rangle$ 
367	an equivalence class. If $f : T\to S$ is a morphism of 
368	locally noetherian schemes, then pulling back the quotient
369	$q: \oplus^r{\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_S} \to \F$ 
370	under $\id\times f : \PP^n_T \to \PP^n_S$
371	defines a family $f^*(q) : \oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n_T} \to f^*(\F)$ over $T$, 
372	which makes sense as tensor product is right-exact and preserves flatness. 
373	The operation of pulling back respects equivalence of families,
374	therefore it gives rise to a contravariant functor
375	$\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$ from the category of
376	all locally noetherian schemes to the category of sets, 
377	by putting 
378	$$\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}(S) = 
379	\{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }S\}$$
380	
381	
382	It is immediate that the functor $\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$
383	satisfies faithfully flat descent. It was proved by
384	Grothendieck that in fact the above functor
385	is representable on the category of all locally noetherian schemes 
386	by a scheme $\quot_{\oplus^r{\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$.

398	The above functors $\Hilb_{\PP^n}$ and $\Quot_{\oplus^r {\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}}$
399	admit the following simple generalisations.

402	Let $Sch_S$ denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes

418	an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
419	by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,

421	well-defined, which gives
422	a set-valued contravariant functor 
423	$\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets$ under which

814	For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$, 
815	consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
816	$X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
817	entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$. 
818	Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
819	$x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically 
820	isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$. 
821	
822	For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let 
823	$P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
824	where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
825	Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
826	the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
827	the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
828	on $U^I_J$. 
829	
830	For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism 
831	$\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
832	defined as follows. The images of the variables 
833	$x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
834	$\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$.
835	In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
836	so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$.
837	
838	Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and 
839	we leave it to the reader to verify that 
840	for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of 
841	cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition 
842	$\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
843	is satisfied. Therefore the schemes 
844	$U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the 
845	${r\choose d}$ different 
846	subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
847	can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
848	finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
849	isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
850	$\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$.
851	
852	
853	
854	\medskip
855	
856	\textbf{Separatedness }
857	The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
858	can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ 
859	defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, 
860	and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme.
861	 
862	
863	\medskip
864	
865	\textbf{Properness }
866	We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. It is enough to
867	verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
868	Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
869	$\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by 
870	a ring homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed
871	one such $I$, next choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum,
872	where $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes 
873	the discrete valuation. As $P^I_I = 1$, note that
874	$\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore
875	$f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$ and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$
876	lies in $GL_d(\K)$.
877	
878	Now consider the homomorphism
879	$g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
880	$$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
881	Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
882	and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
883	$\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$. 
884	As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
885	$\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$. 
886	Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
887	factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of
888	schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
889	$\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked
890	separatedness of $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that 
891	$\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. 
892	
893	
894	\medskip
895	
896	
897	
898	\textbf{Universal quotient }
899	We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
900	together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
901	On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism 
902	$u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
903	by the matrix $X^I$. Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
904	for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
905	$(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
906	by putting 
907	$$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$
908	This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$, 
909	so we get a surjective homomorphism 
910	$u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
911	
912	
913	\medskip
914	\textbf{Projective embedding }
915	As $\U$ is given by the transition functions $g_{I,J}$ described above, 
916	the determinant line bundle 
917	$\det(\U)$ is given by the transition functions
918	$\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J \in GL_1(U^I_J)$.
919	For each $I$, we define a global section 
920	$$\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\grass(r,d),\det(\U))$$ 
921	by putting $\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I \in 
922	\Gamma(U^J,{\mathcal O}_{U^J})$ in terms of the trivialization over the open cover
923	$(U^J)$. 
924	We leave it to the reader to verify that the sections $\sigma_I$ form
925	a linear system which is base point free and separates points 
926	relative to $\spec \Z$, and so gives an embedding of 
927	$\grass(r,d)$ into $\PP^m_{\Z}$ where $m = {r\choose d}-1$.
928	This is a closed embedding by the properness of 
929	$\pi:\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$. In particular, $\det(\U)$
930	is a relatively very ample line bundle on $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$.
931	
932	
933	\medskip
934	
935	{\footnotesize
936	\textbf{Note } The $\sigma_I$ are known as the Pl\"ucker coordinates, 
937	and these satisfy certain quadratic polynomials known as the Pl\"ucker 
938	relations, which define the projective image of the Grassmannian.
939	We will not need these facts. }%end of footnotesize 
940	
941	
942	
943	
944	
945	
946	
947

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}

1569	    \item the triple-overlap field \(t' := t'_{I,J,K}\)
1570	      (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}), with compatibility field
1571	      \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) (\cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_fac}) and \(\mathrm{cocycle}\)
1572	      field (\cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_cocycle}).
1573	  \end{itemize}
1574	  The remaining structural fields \(f_{\mathrm{mono}}\) (each inclusion is a
1575	  monomorphism) and \(f_{\mathrm{hasPullback}}\) (the relevant fibre products
1576	  exist) are discharged by instance synthesis from the open-immersion property of
1577	  the inclusions. Assembling these named fields into the
1578	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\) record yields the datum from which the glued scheme
1579	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is formed.
1580	\end{definition}
1581	
1582	\begin{definition}
1583	
1584	\leanok
1585	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1586	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
1587	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
1588	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle,
1589	    def:gr_chart_overlap, def:gr_chart_incl, lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion,
1590	    lem:gr_chartIncl_self_isIso, def:gr_chart_transition,
1591	    lem:gr_chartTransition_self, def:gr_away_pullback_iso,
1592	    lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst, lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd,
1593	    def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij,
1594	    def:gr_chart_transition', lem:gr_chartTransition'_fac,
1595	    lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq,
1596	    lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id, lem:gr_chartTransition'_cocycle}
1597	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
1598	  % affine patches" (read from
1599	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
1600	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1601	  % "Therefore the schemes
1602	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
1603	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
1604	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
1605	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
1606	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
1607	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
1608	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
1609	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1610	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
1611	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
1612	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
1613	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
1614	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
1615	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
1616	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
1617	  \[
1618	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
1619	  \]
1620	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
1621	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
1622	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
1623	
1624	  \emph{Construction of the glue datum.} The gluing is carried out through the
1625	  Mathlib vehicle for scheme gluing, a glue datum in the sense of a category of
1626	  schemes (the structure \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\), extending the abstract
1627	  glue-datum interface). Its data are supplied as follows. The index set is the
1628	  set \(J := \{\, I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\} : \#(I) = d \,\}\) of size-\(d\)
1629	  subsets. The objects are the charts \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}); the
1630	  pairwise overlaps are the schemes \(U^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_overlap}); the
1631	  inclusions \(f_{I,J} : U^I_J \to U^I\) are the open immersions
1632	  \(\cref{def:gr_chart_incl}\) (open immersions by
1633	  \cref{lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion}, in particular monomorphisms), with the
1634	  identity field \(f_{\mathrm{id}}\) supplied by
1635	  \cref{lem:gr_chartIncl_self_isIso}; the transition isomorphisms
1636	  \(t_{I,J} : U^I_J \to U^J_I\) are the scheme-level transitions
1637	  \(\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}\), with identity field \(t_{\mathrm{id}}\)
1638	  supplied by \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition_self}.
1639	
1640	  The triple-overlap field \(t'\) and its first coherence obligation are supplied
1641	  by the two preceding results. The field itself is the morphism
1642	  \(t'_{I,J,K} : U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K \to U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I\) of
1643	  \cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}, and the compatibility field
1644	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) -- that \(t'\) is compatible with the projections to the
1645	  charts -- is \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_fac}, which reduces (both fibre
1646	  products being affine) to the ring identity
1647	  \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity} via the leg lemmas
1648	  \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst} and \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd}.
1649	
1650	  \emph{Cocycle field.} The \(t'\)-cocycle coherence
1651	  \(t'_{I,J,K} \circ t'_{J,K,I} \circ t'_{K,I,J} = \mathrm{id}\)
1652	  follows from \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_cocycle} via the ring identity
1653	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id}. With this field in hand the glue datum is
1654	  complete: it is the structure \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\) with
1655	  \(U := \texttt{affineChart}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}),
1656	  \(V := \texttt{chartOverlap}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_overlap}),
1657	  \(f := \texttt{chartIncl}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_incl}),
1658	  \(t := \texttt{chartTransition}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}),
1659	  \(t_{\mathrm{id}} := \texttt{chartTransition\_self}\)
1660	  (\cref{lem:gr_chartTransition_self}),
1661	  \(t' := \texttt{chartTransition'}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}),
1662	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}} := \texttt{chartTransition'\_fac}\)
1663	  (\cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_fac}), and the cocycle field above. The glued
1664	  scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is then the colimit
1665	  \((\text{glue datum}).\mathrm{glued}\) produced by the gluing machine.
1666	
1667	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
1668	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
1669	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
1670	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
1671	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
1672	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
1673	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
1674	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
1675	  follows.
1676	\end{definition}
1677
